schools.summary: Bede: the ship a figure of the Church among the nations; the soils allegorised (the way = a mind open to bad thoughts, birds = devils); Augustine: the blindness deserved yet merciful.
Chrysostom: Christ went out to us in the flesh; the Sower's love commends all three; unlike natural soil, stony ground may become fertile, giving hope of repentance.
Robertson on the boat-pulpit and pressing crowds; Henry: the ship a presage of the gospel sent to the Gentile isles.

debates.question: Why does Christ teach 'those without' in parables that they may not perceive?

sides.position: A deserved judicial blindness, yet merciful, that they might know their sin
The Sower's love still gives hope of repentance to all soils

schools.summary: Robertson: the imperfect 'was teaching'; Mark gives four of the day's parables (the rest abound); 'in his teaching' (Ellicott).
Wesley: a parable is any instructive speech illustrating spiritual by natural (Proverbs 1:6); the literal kills, the spiritual gives life.
Henry: parables tempt men to hear and lodge truth in memory, the 'dark lantern' bright to the diligent, a flash to the trifler.
The emphatic call to attention (Wesley: a loud voice); 'the sower' with the article; Gill identifies him as Christ preaching, and any faithful minister.
Chrysostom: 'went out to sow' marks the deliberate impartial casting of the same seed on all; the figure of sowing (vs the planted vine, Isaiah 5) shows obedience now easier.
Lightfoot on the sowing custom (hand or perforated cart); Clarke notes 'of the air' likely a gloss from Luke 8:5; the way-side = the careless, accidental hearer.
Henry: the trodden, unhumbled heart, where the devil like the birds snatches the word; drive them away as Abram did (Genesis 15:11).
Lightfoot on rocky soil with a thin turf; the seed springs fast but cannot strike deep — the shallow, emotional hearer.
Bede: 'stone' = a wanton mind's hardness; breasts delighted with the word's sweetness for a time but with too little healthful desire to conceive the seed of life.
Henry: much affected for the present, a mere flash like crackling thorns, no abiding benefit.
The sun = the heat of persecution that pierces shallow, speculative knowledge; the withering for want of root (Psalm 1:4).
Bede: 'the sun' = raging persecution, against which the stony places have no strength to root.
Henry: undeep impressions wear off in trial, like footprints gone at the next high tide of persecution.
Robertson on Mark's sharper 'throttled' and the unique 'yielded no fruit'; the thorny ground = those eaten up with worldly cares, riches, and lusts.
Bede: riches as thorns that pierce and wound the soul, the desire of them choking the word's entrance.
Henry: the most dangerous because least visible — a barren profession secretly choked, never thrown off yet never fruitful.
Ellicott: Mark's ascending scale (thirty to a hundred); Robertson on the unique 'growing and increasing' — it kept yielding as it grew, fruit being what matters.
Henry: no good fruit but from good seed on good ground, abounding to a hundredfold (Genesis 26:12); only one soil in four bears fruit.
The solemn closing call, spoken to all the sorts of hearers; Clarke notes the addition 'whoso understandeth, let him understand.'
Henry: the formula brackets the parable; Christ's words command attention even before we understand them, that we may find more in them than first appeared.
Cyril of Jerusalem applies it to the baptized, whose ears were anointed to hear the divine mysteries.
Robertson: 'only in Mark, a vivid recollection of Peter'; more than the Twelve present (Ellicott, Clarke); they sought privately not to show their ignorance to the crowd.
Henry: it was good to be near Christ; the teachable withdraw from the crowd to seek the meaning from the Master.
Robertson on mystērion as a permanent possession, discipleship an initiation; Lightfoot: 'those without' = the Gentiles' name now applied to unbelieving Jews; the hostile uninitiated lack the key.
Henry: the mystery is given — both light and sight received from Christ (Luke 24:45).
Wesley: 'without' = obstinate unbelievers who shall not enter the kingdom.
Ellicott: a foreseen result expressed as intention (Hebrew idiom); Robertson notes the irony and that Matthew softens 'that' to 'because' — judgment on wilful blindness.
Calvin: the word blinds only 'by accident,' as sunshine dims weak eyes — the hardening belongs to their own depravity, not the word, whose true end is reconciliation.
Wesley: judicial abandonment — given up to the blindness they had chosen.

debates.question: Does Christ speak in parables in order to blind, or only confirming a self-chosen blindness?

sides.position: A foreseen result of their wilful rejection, expressed as purpose (Hebrew idiom); blindness 'by accident' of their own depravity
A direct judicial purpose of condemnation on the hostile
holders: the plain sense of the Isaiah quotation

schools.summary: Peculiar to Mark; Robertson: surprise at their dulness; Ellicott: the Sower the pattern and key for interpreting all parables.
Henry: a double rebuke — if they miss the plainest, how the darker (Jeremiah 12:5)?; this parable is the key to all the rest, and ignorance must first be felt.
Robertson: Mark names the seed as 'the word' more plainly than the others; Gill expounds the word of life, peace, faith, righteousness, salvation in Christ.
Henry: the word sown impartially on all soils (Isaiah 32:20); Christ the first Sower, then his ministers, looking to God who gives the seed (2 Cor 9:10).
Robertson: Mark's 'where the word is sown,' 'Satan' (vs the evil one / the devil), 'sown within them'; Ellicott on the better text; Clarke: those who 'receive the word negligently.'
Henry: the devil busy about careless hearers as birds about exposed seed; the word snatched 'immediately' from the trodden heart.
Gill: the stony ground = constant hearers with some affection but unbroken hearts, not brought to a sense of sin and need of Christ, their stony hearts not exchanged for hearts of flesh.
The mark of this soil is the very speed and gladness of reception — eager but rootless, springing up fast to wither as fast.
Gill: the word roots only in the natural affections, not the heart — speculative notions and flashy affections, no heart-work.
Henry: the ruin of hypocrites is having no root, no living fixed principle; they endure 'but for a time' and are offended when the word costs them.
Gill: the thorny ground = worldly-disposed hearers, anxious for the things of this life, bent on riches and sensual appetites.
The most subtle failure — neither snatched nor scorched but slowly strangled by a worldly growth springing from the same soil.
Robertson on 'the lusts of other things' (Mark's phrase) — worldly passions drowning the spirit, yet epithumia not evil in itself; Gill on the three thorns of care, riches, and desire.
Wesley: any desire not leading to happiness in God tends to barrenness; beware what 'enters in' after the word is received.
Robertson on 'bear fruit' and Mark's ascending order; Gill: the good ground made good by grace, none being good naturally.
Henry: the fruit is a gospel temper and life, Christian graces and duties; no good fruit but from good seed on a heart made good by new-creating grace.
Chrysostom: the candle of the intellect, fed by meditation; Theophylact: be a candle on the eminence of a godly life, not under the bed of idleness or bushel of appetite.

Bede: the bushel = love of this transitory life, the bed = the body; he sets the light on a candlestick who employs his body in the word's ministry; Pseudo-Jerome: the apostles the candlestick enlightened by the Lord.
Henry: gifts make a man the candle of the Lord (Proverbs 20:27); many hide their light under bed or bushel, but they should shine for God's glory and others' good.
Bede: be not ashamed of the gospel but raise its light amid persecution, looking to the day all is revealed; Pseudo-Jerome: the secret parable comes to light when the Lord speaks it.
Chrysostom: a careful life cannot be obscured by accusation; the upright light cannot finally be hidden.
Henry: gifts are lodged to be communicated; the parables expounded privately that the disciples might teach publicly — taught, that they might teach.
Bede: true hearing is the whole man — ear to search, hands to fulfil, tongue to preach; not turning to fables.
A recurring summons (as at v.9) pressing attentive, obedient hearing; the careless gain nothing, the diligent are admitted deeper.
Theophylact: the measure of application is the measure of profit; Bede: diligent doing and teaching draws God's mercy to give higher sense and reward.
Henry: 'take heed what ye hear' — consider, prove, hold fast the good; the measure is reciprocal (God deals as we deal) and increasing (gifts grow by exercise).
Pseudo-Jerome: faith gains virtue, obedience gains understanding; their lack loses all; Pseudo-Chrysostom: given to him who desires to hear, taken from him who does not.
Chrysostom: the neglectful 'hath not' because he has not truth, yet thinks he has, holding a lie.
Henry: if we do not use, we lose; burying a talent is forfeiture, gifts rust for want of wearing.
Robertson: a parable peculiar to Mark; the seed the word, the growth God's hidden work the sower neither sees nor causes.
Henry: the gospel produces wonderful effects without noise; the growth a mystery like the wind or the change of heart (1 Timothy 3:16); the preacher contributes nothing to it.
The sower sleeps and rises through his ordinary days, never thinking of the corn, yet it grows 'he knoweth not how' — beyond his understanding and power.
Henry: the preacher contributes nothing; the Spirit carries the work on while he sleeps; the growth a mystery he sees but cannot explain.
'Of herself' = without the sower's labour, by God's power; the three stages (blade, ear, full corn) mark the settled order of growth.
Henry: grace grows gradually like nature ('nothing abruptly'), from a tender blade to full corn, small beginnings ripening surely and insuperably.
The harvest echoes Joel 3:13 and Revelation 14:15; 'immediately' the gathering follows the ripening.
Henry: a double harvest — Christ gathers the fruit of holy lives now (John 4:35) and the ripened saints into his barn at the end (Matthew 13:30).
The Gloss: the parable shows the gospel's excellence above all doctrines; Pseudo-Jerome reads the seed as charity, greatest of herbs (1 John 4:16).
Theophylact and Chrysostom: the brief word of faith spread over the earth, sheltering the nations and the wise of the Gentiles; nothing greater than this truth.
Robertson: the smallness 'less than all seeds' is proverbial hyperbole for the kingdom's pervasive expansive power (Matthew 17:20); Gill: the seed = gospel, church, or grace, humble in beginning.
Cyril: faith like the mustard seed, small but fiery, working the greatest effects in the soul.
Gill traces the gospel's unstoppable spread (Acts 12:24) despite opposition; Robertson on Mark's birds 'under the shadow' (vs Matthew's branches); the prophesied growth (Daniel 2:44).
Theophylact (on v.30): the great branches the apostles dividing to Rome, India, and the world; the birds contemplative men finding rest.
Mark records only a few of many parables; Robertson: the veiled parabolic form the only feasible one at this stage (John 16:12); Barnes: leading them by degrees like children.
Wesley and Clarke: a homiletical rule — adapt teaching to the hearers' capacity, the most useful preacher fitting his phrase to the people.

themes: as they were able to hear

schools.summary: Robertson on epilyō (to loosen, explain, even reveal); Ellicott links it to 2 Peter 1:20; Barnes: the method insinuated truth gradually past their prejudices.
Clarke: outside hearers have only superficial knowledge; in the fellowship of the saints all is made plain, the secret of the Lord with them that fear him.
Robertson: after a busy day, the evening crossing a refreshing escape from the crowds; Remigius: the ship one of Christ's three refuges from the multitude.
Pseudo-Jerome and Bede read the ship as the Church bound by the wood of the cross, the sleeping Lord, the storm as devils and the Passion, the calm as the Church's peace.
Henry: Christ crosses to lose no time for work on the other side; the ship used for him cannot sink; they put to sea only after sending the multitude satisfied away.
Ellicott: 'even as he was' marks Christ's utter exhaustion (cf. John 4:6); the disciples take him without preparation; the 'other ships' a detail only in Mark.
Henry: taken without a cloak (not over-nice about the body); the other ships carried those who would venture with Christ — following the Lamb wherever he goes.
Robertson on lailaps, the cyclonic gust falling from Hermon onto the deep lake; the imperfect 'were beating,' the boat 'now filling' — graphic peril; Ellicott on the continuous action.
Henry: a tempest of wind with rain filling the little ship; it is no new thing for the ship carrying Christ and his gospel to be endangered.
The stern, the cushion, Christ worn out with toil; 'carest thou not that we perish?' is Mark's alone, betraying the want of faith reproved.
Chrysostom: he sleeps to give time for their fear and to teach humility; Theophylact: that they might experience his power in themselves.
Henry: he sleeps to try faith and stir prayer; when Christ seems asleep he does not sleep (Psalm 121:4); their harsh cry the fruit of fright.

themes: sleep as a trial of faith

schools.summary: Barnes and Clarke: the majestic word proves his deity — none but God could still the sea by a word; Ellicott: 'be silenced,' the sea a rebel power, the wind lulled.
The Gloss: the King restraining the winds by threat; Theophylact: stilling the sea by his bare word (not Moses' rod or the ark), the disciples thought him truly God.
Henry: 'Peace, be still' a command to our unruly passions (Isaiah 57:20) and a comfort that Christ can calm any storm with a word; commanding the sea is God's prerogative (Jeremiah 31:35).
Robertson: they had the Lord of wind and waves with them yet feared; they had not drawn all the conclusions from his Messiahship; Clarke: fearful though he was with them.
Henry: Mark heightens the reproof ('so fearful,' 'no faith'); not that they lacked faith, but fear eclipsed it when needed; to think Christ careth not is faith asleep.
Robertson: 'they feared a great fear,' with marvel; 'Who then is this?' marks their growth in apprehending Christ, with much yet to learn.
Henry: their fear rectified by faith — fearing the seas less and Christ more, a fear with pleasure in it (like Jonah's mariners, Jonah 1:16); 'What manner of man?' a confession he is more than man.
